ui.keyboardHint: Finish each lesson quiz set to unlock the next lesson.
ui.heroTitle: Learn Java, Kotlin, the JVM, JPA, and Spring by reading real code paths.
ui.heroBody: This track covers Java and Kotlin foundations relevant to Spring, JVM object behavior, bean creation, MVC flow, validation, JPA mappings, transaction boundaries, security, caching, messaging, and testing layers. The goal is to learn Spring as a stack rather than as a list of annotations.
ui.lessonComplete: Lesson complete. The next lesson is now unlocked.
ui.incorrectReasonFallback: That choice does not match what the code or concept is doing.
ui.workspaceNote: This editor is for reading and tweaking the example. Use the external workspace for full execution and larger experiments.
ui.outputReady: Read the code, then verify your answer in the quiz.
ui.outputPlaceholder: Output is shown by the lesson metadata below.
ui.howItWorks1: Open a lesson and read the explanation next to a focused code sample.
ui.howItWorks2: Use the embedded editor to annotate or tweak the snippet while keeping the lesson context nearby.
ui.howItWorks3: Finish the quiz set, then continue in the external workspace when you want a larger sandbox.
ui.previewStatus: Move from entry points and DI through MVC, persistence, transactions, security, and operational boundaries.
ui.homeTip: Use the embedded editor as a scratchpad, then continue in Kotlin Playground or your IDE for a full Spring project.

01_java_main.title: Java Main and Static Entry
01_java_main.description:
A Java program starts from `public static void main(String[] args)`.

```java
public static void main(String[] args)
```

The JVM needs an entry point it can call before any object exists.

q2.question: Why is `main` declared `static`?
options: The JVM must call it before any `Main` instance exists.
Static methods are always faster.
Without `static`, Java cannot print.
explanations: Correct. The entry point must be callable without constructing the class first.
Performance is not the reason.
Printing is unrelated to `static`.
q2.answerReason: The JVM discovers and invokes `main` before your application has created objects.

q3.question: What does `String[] args` represent?
options: A list of loaded classes.
The command-line arguments passed to the program.
The fields declared on the class.
explanations: Loaded classes are unrelated.
Correct. `args` carries process input from the command line.
Fields are unrelated to the method parameter.
q3.answerReason: `args` is just the process argument array provided at startup.

02_kotlin_data_class.title: Kotlin Data Classes
02_kotlin_data_class.description:
Kotlin `data class` generates value-oriented methods from constructor properties.

```kotlin
data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)
```

q2.question: Why use `data class` here?
options: It generates useful value-style methods such as `copy`, `equals`, and `toString`.
It automatically registers a Spring bean.
It makes the object mutable by default.
explanations: Correct. `data class` is a language feature for value-centric types.
Bean registration is separate.
Mutability depends on `val` vs `var`.
q2.answerReason: Data classes are for state-holder types where field-based equality and copying are useful.

q3.question: Which generated feature is especially useful for immutable updates?
options: `hashCode()`
`componentN()`
`copy()`
explanations: `hashCode()` is for hashing, not structural updates.
`componentN()` is for destructuring.
Correct. `copy()` makes small immutable updates ergonomic.
q3.answerReason: `copy()` is one reason Kotlin immutable data modeling feels concise.

03_jvm_memory.title: Stack Frames, References, and Heap Objects
03_jvm_memory.description:
A local reference is not the same thing as the object it points to.

```java
Box b = a;
```

That assignment copies a reference, not the heap object itself.

q2.question: Why does changing `b.value` affect `a.value`?
options: Both variables refer to the same object instance.
Java deep-copies object fields on assignment.
The JVM merges the variable names.
explanations: Correct. Two references point to one object.
Java does not deep-copy objects on assignment.
Compilation does not merge names like that.
q2.answerReason: Assignment of object references copies the reference, not the object.

q3.question: What is actually copied by `Box b = a;`?
options: A new heap object with duplicated fields.
The reference value pointing to the object.
Only the `value` field.
explanations: Simple assignment does not clone the object.
Correct. The object stays one object; the reference is copied.
Java does not copy only one field here.
q3.answerReason: This is why aliasing and mutation through shared references matter on the JVM.

04_spring_di.title: Constructor Injection

q2.question: Why is constructor injection usually preferred?
options: It makes required dependencies explicit and easier to test.
It lets Spring ignore missing dependencies.
It removes the need for interfaces.
explanations: Correct. Constructor injection works well with explicit object design.
Missing required dependencies are still errors.
Interfaces are unrelated.
q2.answerReason: If a class cannot work without a dependency, the constructor is the clearest place to require it.

q3.question: Why does constructor injection fit immutable service design well?
options: It makes fields mutable later.
Dependencies can be required once and stored in fixed fields.
It prevents bean registration.
explanations: Constructor injection usually works with final/immutable dependencies.
Correct. The object starts life fully wired.
Beans are still registered normally.
q3.answerReason: You declare the object’s contract up front instead of hoping fields are injected later.

05_bean_lifecycle.title: Bean Creation and Container Ownership

q2.question: What does it mean for Spring to “manage” a bean?
options: It compiles the class into the JVM.
It removes the need for constructors.
The container controls creation, wiring, and lifecycle of that object.
explanations: Compilation is separate from the container.
Constructors still matter.
Correct. Bean management is about object lifecycle and dependency graph control.
q2.answerReason: Dependency injection works because the container, not arbitrary calling code, controls object construction.

q3.question: Why does container-managed creation matter architecturally?
options: Because wiring can be centralized and cross-cutting lifecycle behavior can be applied consistently.
Because beans no longer need types.
Because it makes persistence automatic for every class.
explanations: Correct. Centralized construction enables consistent configuration and lifecycle control.
Type information still matters.
Persistence is a separate concern.
q3.answerReason: A container becomes useful when object graphs and cross-cutting setup stop being trivial.

06_web_controller.title: Controller to Service Flow
06_web_controller.description:
A controller should stay thin: receive the HTTP request, delegate business logic, and shape the response.

```java
@GetMapping("/users/{id}")
UserResponse get(...)
```

q2.question: What is the better responsibility split?
options: Controller handles transport details; service owns business logic.
Controller should contain all SQL.
Service should parse HTTP annotations directly.
explanations: Correct. Keep transport and domain concerns separated.
Putting SQL in controllers creates tight coupling.
HTTP parsing belongs to the web boundary.
q2.answerReason: Thin controllers scale better because business rules remain reusable and testable.

q3.question: What usually belongs in the controller rather than the service?
options: The core pricing algorithm.
Entity lifecycle mapping.
HTTP-specific concerns like path variables and response status.
explanations: Business rules are usually more reusable in services.
Entity mapping belongs to persistence/domain concerns.
Correct. Controllers are the transport boundary.
q3.answerReason: The controller is where HTTP concepts enter and leave the system.

07_jpa_entity.title: Entity Identity and Persistence Context

q1.question: What is printed before persistence assigns an id?

q2.question: Why is entity identity important?
options: The persistence context tracks one managed instance per database identity.
Entities are just DTOs with annotations.
Identity matters only for native SQL.
explanations: Correct. JPA change tracking and caching depend on identity.
Entities have lifecycle semantics beyond DTOs.
Identity is core to ORM behavior too.
q2.answerReason: If two objects claim one row identity, the persistence context must reason about one managed instance.

q3.question: What changes when an entity becomes managed?
options: It loses its identity field.
JPA begins tracking changes on that instance for flush or commit.
It can no longer be compared by identity.
explanations: Managed entities still have identity.
Correct. Managed means the persistence context observes that object as part of the unit of work.
Identity remains important.
q3.answerReason: Managed entities participate in change tracking because the persistence context now owns that live instance relationship.

08_lazy_loading.title: Lazy Loading and Object Graph Boundaries
08_lazy_loading.description:
ORM relationships are often loaded lazily so the whole graph is not fetched immediately.

```java
member.getOrders()
```

That convenience can hide extra queries if the access pattern is not designed carefully.

q2.question: Why is lazy loading attractive?
options: It eliminates all database queries.
It forces everything into one giant join.
It delays fetching related data until code actually touches it.
explanations: Queries still happen; they are just delayed.
Lazy loading is often used specifically to avoid eager loading everything.
Correct. The idea is to fetch related data on demand.
q2.answerReason: Lazy loading is about delaying work, not eliminating it.

q3.question: Why can lazy loading become a performance problem?
options: Touching related objects in loops can trigger many extra queries unexpectedly.
It prevents entities from having fields.
It disables transactions automatically.
explanations: Correct. The classic risk is hidden N+1 query behavior.
Fields are unaffected.
Transactions are separate.
q3.answerReason: What looks like object navigation in application code may actually be repeated database traffic.

09_transaction_boundary.title: Transaction Boundaries

q2.question: Why use `@Transactional` around related writes?
options: To keep the unit of work atomic if one step fails.
To make methods public automatically.
To avoid JDBC connections.
explanations: Correct. The goal is all-or-nothing state change.
Visibility is unrelated.
Transactions do not remove the need for a database connection.
q2.answerReason: Atomicity matters when several writes should commit or roll back together.

q3.question: What failure does a transaction primarily protect against?
options: Typos in variable names.
The JVM loading classes in the wrong order.
A partial commit where some writes escape before a later step fails.
explanations: Transactions do not solve source-level typing mistakes.
Class loading order is unrelated here.
Correct. Partial persistence is the real business risk.
q3.answerReason: Without a transaction, the system may persist an inconsistent half-completed business action.

10_repository_queries.title: Repository Abstractions and Query Boundaries

q2.question: Why is a repository abstraction useful?
options: It removes the need for a database.
It separates domain-oriented access operations from higher-level business logic.
It turns every query into a transaction automatically.
explanations: A repository still talks to a data source somehow.
Correct. Services can ask for domain data without owning low-level query details.
Transactions are separate.
q2.answerReason: Repositories can keep persistence concerns from leaking through every service method.

q3.question: What is the risk when services build ad hoc queries everywhere?
options: Persistence logic becomes scattered and harder to evolve consistently.
The JVM stops supporting collections.
Controllers no longer compile.
explanations: Correct. Scattered data access creates coupling and inconsistency.
Collections are unaffected.
Compilation is not the main issue.
q3.answerReason: A dedicated data-access boundary helps contain change in how data is fetched and persisted.

11_spring_test.title: Slice Tests and Fast Feedback
11_spring_test.description:
Not every test needs the full application context. Slice tests load only the layer you care about.

```java
@WebMvcTest(UserController.class)
```

q2.question: Why use a slice test such as `@WebMvcTest`?
options: It narrows the loaded context to the web layer for faster, focused tests.
It automatically tests the real transaction log.
It replaces assertions with Spring-generated checks.
explanations: Correct. Focused scope speeds feedback and isolates failures.
A web slice test does not load the whole database stack by default.
You still write assertions yourself.
q2.answerReason: Good test pyramids rely on many fast, narrow tests before full integration checks.

q3.question: Why are focused tests valuable even when you also have integration tests?
options: They replace all need for integration tests.
They isolate one layer and fail faster with a narrower cause.
They guarantee every bean is loaded.
explanations: Broader tests still matter.
Correct. Narrow tests keep failure reasons clearer and feedback faster.
Slice tests intentionally do not load every bean.
q3.answerReason: Fast focused tests catch many issues before you pay the cost of starting the whole application.

12_testcontainers_boundary.title: Integration Tests and Real Infrastructure
12_testcontainers_boundary.description:
Some tests should cross the abstraction boundary and exercise real infrastructure.

```text
controller -> service -> repository -> real database
```

The point is not to replace narrow tests, but to verify behavior at the seams where mocks can lie.

q2.question: Why keep some real integration tests even if slice tests are strong?
options: Because integration tests make unit tests unnecessary.
Because mocks are always wrong.
Because some failures appear only when the real framework, configuration, and infrastructure interact together.
explanations: Integration tests complement rather than eliminate narrow tests.
Mocks are useful, just not sufficient for every risk.
Correct. Real seams can fail in ways isolated tests do not expose.
q2.answerReason: Abstraction is good, but some risks live exactly at the integration boundary.

q3.question: What is the main tradeoff of deeper integration tests?
options: They give broader confidence but are slower and usually less specific when they fail.
They always run faster than pure unit tests.
They remove the need to understand framework configuration.
explanations: Correct. Broader scope means broader signals and higher cost.
Integration tests are usually slower.
They often require even more configuration awareness.
q3.answerReason: Use integration tests deliberately for seam validation, not as the only testing strategy.

13_transaction_isolation.title: Transaction Isolation and Concurrency
13_transaction_isolation.description:
Transactions are not only about atomicity; isolation levels affect what concurrent work can observe of each other.

```text
concurrent transactions -> visibility rules -> anomalies or guarantees
```

q2.question: Why does transaction isolation matter?
options: Because concurrent transactions may otherwise observe or interfere with each other in surprising ways.
Because isolation changes Kotlin syntax.
Because it removes the need for indexes.
explanations: Correct. Concurrency anomalies are a transaction concern.
Language syntax is unrelated.
Indexes are separate.
q2.answerReason: Transactional correctness includes what concurrent units of work can see of one another.

q3.question: What is the tradeoff around stronger isolation?
options: It always improves performance.
It may improve correctness guarantees while also increasing contention or cost.
It disables persistence entirely.
explanations: Stronger isolation can add cost.
Correct. Stronger guarantees often come with coordination overhead.
Persistence still exists.
q3.answerReason: Database guarantees are usually a tradeoff between correctness strength and concurrency cost.

14_validation_boundary.title: Validation at the Boundary
14_validation_boundary.description: Input validation checks whether incoming data satisfies application rules before deeper processing. Boundary validation narrows the set of states inner layers must handle.

q2.question: Why validate input near the request boundary?
options: To reject malformed or invalid data before deeper layers assume it is trustworthy.
To make persistence unnecessary.
To avoid ever writing tests.
explanations: Correct. Validation protects deeper logic from bad assumptions.
Persistence is unrelated.
Validation does not replace testing.
q2.answerReason: Boundary validation narrows the set of states inner layers must handle.

q3.question: Why separate transport validation from deeper business rules?
options: Because validation only matters in static websites.
Because business logic should never inspect any input.
Because transport-format checks and domain rules are related but not always owned by the same layer.
explanations: Validation matters broadly.
Business logic still depends on valid inputs.
Correct. Different layers may own different kinds of correctness checks.
q3.answerReason: Not every rule belongs in the same layer just because both are called validation.

15_security_filters.title: Security Filters and Request Protection
15_security_filters.description:
Security is often enforced by filters or middleware before the application reaches business endpoints.

```text
request -> security filter chain -> controller
```

q2.question: Why is security often implemented in request filters or middleware?
options: Because controllers cannot read headers.
Because authentication and authorization are cross-cutting concerns that should apply consistently before business logic runs.
Because security removes the need for user models.
explanations: Controllers can read headers.
Correct. Security is a boundary concern that should be applied consistently.
User models may still matter.
q2.answerReason: Access control belongs near the request boundary, not sprinkled through business code.

q3.question: What is a danger of ad hoc security checks scattered through controllers?
options: Inconsistent protection and missed paths can appear as the codebase grows.
The JVM stops authenticating users.
Annotations stop existing.
explanations: Correct. Security that is not centralized is easier to forget or misapply.
The JVM does not authenticate users for you.
Annotations still exist.
q3.answerReason: Cross-cutting concerns are risky when every endpoint must remember them manually.

16_caching_basic.title: Caching and Stale Data Tradeoffs
16_caching_basic.description:
Caching can reduce repeated expensive work, but it introduces freshness and invalidation problems.

```text
source of truth -> cache -> readers
```

q2.question: Why use caching in an application stack?
options: To eliminate all state.
To avoid every database forever.
To reuse expensive results and reduce repeated load on slower components.
explanations: Caching does not eliminate state.
Databases still exist.
Correct. Caching is a performance tool for repeated work.
q2.answerReason: Caching is often about shifting load and latency, not deleting the original source of truth.

q3.question: What is the hardest part of caching conceptually?
options: Writing `if` statements.
Keeping cached data coherent enough with the source of truth as data changes.
Compiling the project.
explanations: Branching syntax is easy compared with coherence.
Correct. Invalidation and freshness rules are the hard part.
Compilation is unrelated.
q3.answerReason: Caching is valuable precisely because correctness and freshness are not free.

17_messaging_async.title: Messaging and Async Boundaries
17_messaging_async.description: Queues and message brokers let parts of a system communicate asynchronously and decouple request-time work from later processing.

q2.question: Why use messaging between parts of a system?
options: To decouple producers and consumers in time and smooth certain workloads asynchronously.
To remove all failure handling.
To make databases unnecessary.
explanations: Correct. Messaging is often about decoupling and asynchronous coordination.
Failure handling still matters.
Data stores may still be needed.
q2.answerReason: Asynchronous boundaries help when immediate synchronous coupling is too rigid or too expensive.

q3.question: What is a common tradeoff when introducing messaging?
options: Everything becomes strictly simpler.
You get synchronous request/response semantics everywhere.
You gain decoupling, but also introduce eventual consistency and operational complexity.
explanations: Distributed systems usually do not become simpler automatically.
Messaging is often used because you do not want synchronous coupling everywhere.
Correct. Decoupling and complexity often come together.
q3.answerReason: Asynchronous architecture shifts when and how consistency is achieved.

18_component_scan.title: Component Scan and Stereotypes

q2.question: Why use Spring stereotypes?
options: To let the container discover and register components.
To make the class final.
To remove constructors.
explanations: Correct. Stereotypes mark application components.
Finality is unrelated.
Constructors still exist.
q2.answerReason: Stereotypes are mainly about registration and intent.

q3.question: What does component scanning save you from?
options: Writing all business logic twice.
Manual bean registration for every application class.
Using Java packages.
explanations: It does not duplicate logic.
Correct. Scanning automates registration.
Packages still exist normally.
q3.answerReason: Scanning reduces hand-wired registration work.

19_dto_mapping.title: DTO Mapping at the Boundary
19_dto_mapping.description:
Controllers often return DTOs instead of exposing entities directly.

```java
record UserResponse(Long id, String name) {}
```

The API contract should stay separate from persistence details.

q2.question: Why return DTOs from controllers?
options: Because entities cannot contain fields.
Because API shape and persistence shape should not be tightly coupled.
Because Spring MVC forbids returning entities.
explanations: Entities can contain fields.
Correct. DTOs protect the API boundary.
Spring can return entities, but it is often weak design.
q2.answerReason: DTOs keep transport shape independent from persistence shape.

q3.question: What does DTO mapping control?
options: What data the API exposes and how it evolves.
How the JVM schedules threads.
Whether SQL can run.
explanations: Correct. DTOs define the external contract.
Thread scheduling is unrelated.
SQL still runs either way.
q3.answerReason: DTOs are about boundary design and contract control.

20_proxy_self_invocation.title: Proxy Self-Invocation

q2.question: Why can a self-call skip `@Transactional` behavior?
options: Because transactions only work in Kotlin.
Because proxy interception happens at the bean boundary, not on plain `this` calls.
Because annotations are removed at runtime.
explanations: Kotlin is unrelated.
Correct. The proxy sees external calls.
That is not the reason here.
q2.answerReason: Proxy-based features depend on where interception happens.

q3.question: What is the design lesson?
options: Framework magic has boundaries you need to understand.
Internal method calls are illegal.
Annotations always rewrite bytecode.
explanations: Correct. You need to know where framework behavior begins and ends.
Internal calls are legal.
That is not the default Spring model.
q3.answerReason: AOP-style features are boundary-based, not magic everywhere.

21_fetch_join_tradeoff.title: Fetch Join Tradeoffs
21_fetch_join_tradeoff.description:
Fetch joins can reduce N+1 queries, but they are not a universal fix.

```jpql
select o from Order o join fetch o.lines
```

They trade query count against row duplication and pagination behavior.

q2.question: Why is “add more fetch joins” a weak blanket rule?
options: Because JPQL does not support joins.
Because fetch joins always disable caching.
Because fewer queries can still mean larger result sets or pagination problems.
explanations: JPQL does support joins.
That is not the main issue.
Correct. Query shape still matters.
q2.answerReason: Loading strategy is always a tradeoff, not one universal rule.

q3.question: What should drive the loading strategy?
options: Only fewer lines of code.
The real access pattern and query behavior.
Only the number of repository methods.
explanations: Line count is not the goal.
Correct. Optimize for actual access and query shape.
Method count is not the target.
q3.answerReason: Measure the real access pattern, not one metric in isolation.

22_domain_events.title: Domain Events and Decoupling
22_domain_events.description:
A domain event lets one part of the system publish that something important happened.

```java
publish(new OrderPlaced(orderId));
```

Other flows can react without being hard-wired into the same call chain.

q2.question: Why use domain events?
options: To decouple follow-up actions from the direct request path.
To make transactions unnecessary.
To prevent business logic from existing.
explanations: Correct. Events reduce direct coupling.
Transactions still matter.
Business logic still exists.
q2.answerReason: Events separate the fact that something happened from all immediate reactions.

q3.question: What new cost comes with events?
options: Java stops supporting classes.
Ordering, retries, observability, and consistency get harder.
Entities cannot be saved anymore.
explanations: Classes still work normally.
Correct. Decoupling adds operational tradeoffs.
Persistence still exists.
q3.answerReason: Events shift complexity instead of removing it.

23_saga_compensation.title: Saga and Compensation
23_saga_compensation.description:
Across services, one giant local transaction usually does not exist.

```text
step 1 -> step 2 -> compensate if later work fails
```

A saga models workflow recovery rather than pretending everything rolls back together.

q2.question: Why is a saga different from one local DB transaction?
options: Because sagas run only in tests.
Because sagas disable failure handling.
Because distributed workflows need explicit recovery and compensation.
explanations: Sagas are a production pattern.
Failure handling is the point.
Correct. Cross-service consistency needs workflow reasoning.
q2.answerReason: Distributed work requires step-by-step recovery logic.

q3.question: What should a strong saga design include?
options: Clear compensation rules for partial success.
Only larger controller methods.
No monitoring because the flow is async.
explanations: Correct. Partial completion must be planned.
Controller size is irrelevant.
Async systems need more observability, not less.
q3.answerReason: A saga without recovery rules is incomplete.

24_connection_pool_pressure.title: Connection Pool Pressure
24_connection_pool_pressure.description:
Low CPU does not always mean the service has spare capacity.

```text
request threads -> wait for DB connection
```

If the pool is saturated, throughput is bounded by the downstream resource.

q2.question: What does connection-pool exhaustion usually signal?
options: The JVM forgot how to schedule.
A downstream capacity bottleneck is limiting throughput.
The controller is missing annotations.
explanations: That is not the first reading.
Correct. A shared dependency is saturated.
Annotations are unrelated.
q2.answerReason: The pool is a hard capacity boundary for DB-bound work.

q3.question: Why can CPU still look low?
options: Because SQL uses no resources.
Because low CPU always means the app is healthy.
Because threads are blocked on an external resource rather than burning compute.
explanations: SQL still uses resources.
That inference is unsafe.
Correct. Waiting and useful compute are different states.
q3.answerReason: A blocked system can be unhealthy without being CPU-bound.

25_modular_monolith.title: Modular Monolith Architecture
25_modular_monolith.description:
A modular monolith keeps one deployable unit while enforcing strong internal boundaries.

```text
orders | billing | users
```

That often gives most of the separation value without immediate distributed-system cost.

q2.question: Why can a modular monolith be a strong early architecture choice?
options: It gives internal separation without paying distributed-system complexity too early.
It is always more scalable than every microservice design.
Spring Boot cannot support multiple modules.
explanations: Correct. It matches complexity to current needs.
That is too absolute.
Spring Boot can support modular organization.
q2.answerReason: Distribution should solve a real problem, not be premature structure.

q3.question: What still matters inside a modular monolith?
options: Only one package name.
Clear module APIs and boundaries in the codebase.
Disabling tests because everything is local.
explanations: Package naming alone is not enough.
Correct. Internal architecture still matters.
Local deployment does not remove the need for tests.
q3.answerReason: A monolith can still be well-architected or badly coupled.
